scenarios.task: We use expedited 2-day shipping as our standard for everything we ship, from high-value time-sensitive electronics to bulk low-value replacement parts that customers wouldn't mind waiting a week for. It keeps things simple to have one shipping standard across the board.
criteria: Flags applying one shipping speed/cost standard across very different urgency/value categories as the uniform-shipping-policy failure mode
States that this overspends on genuinely low-urgency goods while the simplicity doesn't offset the real cost difference
Recommends segmenting shipping policy by actual product urgency and value rather than one blanket standard
Notes this isn't an argument for slower shipping everywhere — the point is matching speed to what each category actually needs

scenarios.task: We keep a flat 15% safety stock buffer on every SKU across all our warehouse locations, since it's simple to apply consistently and has generally kept us out of trouble.
criteria: Flags a flat percentage buffer across all SKUs/locations as the arbitrary-safety-stock-levels failure mode
States that the right safety stock level is a function of each product/location's actual demand and lead-time variability, not a fixed rule of thumb
Recommends calculating safety stock statistically from real demand and lead-time variance per product/location combination
Notes that a flat rule predictably produces both unnecessary carrying cost in low-variability cases and stockouts in high-variability ones

scenarios.task: We're switching to a cheaper ocean freight option for a key product line instead of air freight, since the freight cost is significantly lower per unit. I'm recommending the switch based on that cost comparison.
criteria: Flags comparing freight cost alone as the freight-cost-only-mode-selection failure mode
States that total landed cost — including inventory carrying cost over the longer transit time and stockout/service-risk impact — needs to be compared, not freight cost in isolation
Recommends calculating the total landed cost difference between the two options before recommending the switch
Notes the longer, less predictable transit time could offset or exceed the freight savings once carrying cost and service risk are included

scenarios.task: Sales just promised a major new customer 24-hour delivery turnaround nationwide as part of closing the deal. I wasn't consulted before the commitment was made, and I'm not sure our current network capacity can actually support that turnaround consistently across every region.
criteria: Flags the commitment being made without checking it against actual network capacity as the overcommitting-network-capability failure mode
Recommends immediately assessing whether the network can actually support this turnaround before it becomes a customer-facing failure, rather than silently trying to absorb the gap
States that this kind of unchecked commitment predictably surfaces later as a customer-facing service failure instead of an internally managed constraint
Recommends establishing a process where service commitments are checked against network capacity before being made to customers going forward
